content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 12, 2015

Martha O'Connell
3300 Narvaez Ave, Space 31
San Jose, CA 95136

Re:    Your Request for Informal Assistance
       Our File No. I-15-220

Dear Ms. O'Connell:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict
of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as
common law conflicts of interest or Section 1090.

     Because your questions are general in nature, we are treating this request as one for informal
assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an
opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) Recently, you requested
additional advice regarding participation in the San Jose Rent Control Ordinance and the
"budget/fees approval; Mobilehome Rent Ordinance fees." Please note, because the Fair Political
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice
(In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), this letter is based on the facts presented.

QUESTIONS

1. Does the Act prohibit you from participating in all mobilehome matters before the City
   of San Jose Housing and Community Development Commission due to your interest in
   your personal finances relating to your mobilehome?

2. Does the Act prohibit you from participating in the Mobilehome Park Preservation
   Policies/Conversion Ordinance matter?

3. Does the Act prohibit you from participating in Mobilehome Rent Ordinance/
   Mobilehome "Opt-In/Stay in Business" matter?

4. Does the Act prohibit you from participating in "budget/fee approval; Mobilehome Rent
   Ordinance fees" matters?

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-220
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Generally, the Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions related to mobilehome
matters if there is a reasonably foreseeable material effect on your personal finances.
This determination is necessarily fact-specific. Should you need additional assistance
determining whether any particular decision related to mobilehomes may have a
reasonably foreseeable material effect on your personal finances, you should seek
further advice describing the nature of the decision.

2. The Act prohibits you from taking part in the Mobilehome Park Preservation
Policies/Conversion Ordinance because it is reasonably foreseeable that it will have a
material effect on your personal finances.

3. The Act prohibits you from taking part in the Mobilehome Rent Ordinance/
Mobilehome "Opt-In/Stay in Business" matter because it is reasonably foreseeable that
it will have a material effect on your personal finances.

4. Generally, if there is a decision to change mobilehome rent fees, it is reasonably
foreseeable that it will have a material effect on your personal finances. However, we
cannot analyze the potential effect any particular decision may have on your personal
finances unless specific facts are provided.

FACTS

You are a commissioner on the San Jose City Housing and Community Development
Commission. As commissioner, you are a designated employee under the City's Conflict of Interest
Code. You reside in, and own, a mobilehome located on property that you lease on a month-to-
month basis. You, or your mobilehome park, are subject to the Mobilehome Conversion Ordinance,
the Mobilehome Rent Ordinance, and Mobilehome Rent Ordinance fees.

The "Mobilehome Park Preservation Policies/Conversion Ordinance" relates to San Jose
City Council's current goal to strengthen and regulations for the preservation of
existing mobilehome parks as affordable housing. The staff is proposing a new City Council Policy,
General Plan text amendments, and amendments to the Zoning Code to implement this Council
direction. This city-wide effort is in response to several mobilehome park conversions in
neighboring counties, and the Council members' specific concern regarding five mobilehome parks
located in the boundaries of the City's "Urban Villages." Several Council members have noted that
these five mobilehome parks "could be converted." Your mobilehome park is not located in the one
of the City's "Urban Villages" parks, currently considered most at risk.

City staff research shows that the City of San Jose has 59 mobilehome parks with
approximately 10,836 mobilehomes that house approximately 35,000 residents. The City population
is approximately 1,000,536 residents, according to recent census figures. A mobilehome is typically
owned by its occupant and located on rented space in a mobilehome park. Mobilehome parks'

File No. A-15-220
Page No. 3

space-rents are regulated by the City's Mobilehome Rent Control Ordinance. The rent-controlled
mobilehome park spaces provide essential affordable housing to residents.

City staff research states that the impacts of a mobilehome park conversion to another use
could be significant. For example, displaced mobilehome owners would face the cost of moving
their existing mobilehome or finding alternative housing. Moreover, staff research indicates that
most mobilehomes cannot be moved, so displaced mobilehome owners may ultimately lose their
homes if a mobilehome park is converted to another use. You report that you do not know if your
mobilehome can be moved and retain its value.

The San Jose City "Mobilehome Rent Ordinance," regulates rent increases on mobilehomes
and mobilehome lots. The City's Mobilehome Rent Ordinance covers spaces in which the
plumbing, electrical and sewer permits were issued prior to September 7, 1979. Some mobilehome
spaces may be exempted from complying with the Ordinance, including those with government
subsidized rents or long term leases (over 12 months). Under the Mobilehome Rent Ordinance,
annual increases on space rents are set by the City of San Jose at 75% of the San Francisco-Oakland
All Urban Consumers CPI, with a minimum of 3% and a maximum of 7%. Park owners must
request a formal administrative hearing before going over this amount. If the rent on a mobilehome
space was not increased in the prior year, the park may combine the percentages for the two years.
There must be at least 12 months between any two rent increases. Rents may not, with a few
exceptions, be increased when the mobilehome is sold or transferred. The exceptions are
abandonments, an eviction by a court or a dealer pull-out.

The recent agenda you provided contained item (g), the "Mobilehome 'Opt -In/Stay in
Business' Concept." The November 11, 2015 City staff draft states this concept involves
mobilehome park owners agreeing to "remain in business for a minimum of 20 years . . . in
exchange for the ability to pass-through certain capital improvements in addition to obtaining a
restricted vacancy decontrol allowance." Owners that participate in the Opt-In program could assess
residents a monthly capital improvement pass-through fee for all capital improvements deemed
eligible. The fee would be calculated based upon the amount of the capital improvements, amortized
over time and divided by the number of mobile homes in the park. Mobilehome park owners could
also "increase current base rents" when an "in-place transfer" (sale of a home by an existing
resident) occurs. The amount of the base rent increase would be based on the level of capital
investment made by the park owner. The purchaser would also be responsible for any existing
capital investment pass-through.

As to "Mobilehome Rent Ordinance fees," you state that you are subject to such fees; and
that "fees are generally raised by a few dollars each year. No proposal yet." We have no additional
information regarding this issue or other "budget/fee" issues.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.

File No. A-15-220
Page No. 4

A public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect on the official's financial interests and is distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103, emphasis added.)

Financial interests potentially disqualifying under the Act include only those interests
identified in Section 87103. Under this section and Regulation 18700(c)(6), "financial interest"
includes an interest in the public official's own personal finances. Based upon the facts provided,
your interest in your personal finances is the only potentially disqualifying interest implicated by
the decisions in questions.²

In general, your interest in your personal finances does not, in and of itself, disqualify you
from participating in decisions before the Commission. Rather, you would be disqualified only from
decisions that have a foreseeable and material financial effect on your personal financial interests as
discussed below.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Under Regulation 18700(d)(1), we must first consider if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
government decision will have a financial effect on an official's financial interests. Generally, a
financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is "explicitly involved" in a
decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) An interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party
in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official's agency. (Ibid.) A financial
interest is the subject of a governmental decision if it involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial
interest. (Regulation 18701(a).)

Regulation 18701(b) provides that if the interest is not explicitly involved in the government
decision, the financial effect is reasonably foreseeable "if the financial effect can be recognized as a
realistic possibility" and is "more than hypothetical or theoretical." If the financial result cannot be
expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) Factors to consider in determining foreseeability are
provided in Regulation 18701(b) (1) through (6).

Under Regulation 18700(d)(2), if there is a reasonably foreseeable financial effect, we then
consider if the financial effect will be material. For a personal financial interest, a financial effect is
material if the official will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision.
(Regulation 18702.5.) Examples of a material financial effect for the personal finances of an
owner-occupier of a mobilehome with a mobilehome with a monthly rental space include: the increase in or decrease in
the value of the mobilehome; resale value of the mobilehome; increase or decrease in the cost/value
of the space-rental; or costs associated with a move from a particular mobilehome park.³

² Note that an interest in a month-to-month lease is not a potentially disqualifying interest. (Regulation 18233.)

³ Note that an analysis of the effect of a government decision on your financial interest in your personal
finances is fact-specific. Accordingly, we cannot analyze the potential effect any particular decision may have on your
personal finances unless the nature of the decision has been identified. Should you need additional assistance
determining whether any particular decision related to mobilehomes may have a reasonably foreseeable material effect
on your personal finances, you should seek further advice describing the nature of the decision.

File No. A-15-220
Page No. 5

Mobilehome Park Protection Policies/Conversion Ordinance

Given the current facts, the applicable foreseeability standard is whether a financial effect "can be recognized as a realistic possibility," and is "more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation 1870l(b).)

Here, where the City is seeking to strengthen its mobilehome park preservation and conversion policies, it is foreseeable that the City's action could result in changes to its current policies which could impact your mobilehome and your current mobilehome park. As noted above by City staff, the conversion of a mobilehome park most likely results in a mobilehome owner's complete loss of their home, and greater expenses in obtaining new housing. Thus, the policies could have a reasonably foreseeable, measurable financial benefit or loss to you.

Mobilehome Rent Ordinance: Mobilehome "Opt-In/Stay in Business" matter

Policy decisions relating to allowing mobilehome park owners who agree to remain in business for a minimum of 20 years to impose a monthly capital improvement pass-through fee, and/or change the base rents for a purchaser of a mobilehome could foreseeably impact your mobilehome and your current mobilehome park. Thus, the policies could have a reasonably foreseeable, measurable financial benefit or loss to you.

Budget/fee approval; Mobilehome Rent Ordinance fees

We do not have any information regarding a particular "budget/fee" decision such that we can analyze the effect on your personal finances. You have indicated that "fees are generally raised by a few dollars each year. No proposal yet." Your City Attorney has inquired "whether with respect to the budget/fee approval, the impact of the change in Mobilehome Rent Ordinance fees on your personal finances may be considered inconsequential or insignificant such that it does not lead to a conflict."

You would be disqualified from such a fee decision if it has a foreseeable and material financial effect on your personal financial interests. However, Regulation 18702(b) does provide that notwithstanding the materiality standards in Regulations 18702.1 through 18702.5, the financial effect of a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant. Depending on what the impact of the change in fees on your personal finances is, it is possible that it could be considered nominal or inconsequential.

File No. A-15-220
Page No. 6


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: [signature]
L. Karen Harrison
Counsel, Legal Division

LKH:jgl

As to "Mobilehome Rent Ordinance fees," you state that you are subject to such fees; and

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.  
A public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision 
will have a material financial eflecl on the official's financial interests and is distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103, emphasis added.) 
Financial interests potentially disqualifying under the Act include only those interests 
identified in Section 87103. Under this section and Regulation 18700(c)(6), "financial interest" 
includes an interest in the public official's own personal finances. Based upon the facts provided, 
your interest in your personal finances is the only potentially disqualifying interest implicated by 
the decisions in questions.2 
In general, your interest in your personal finances does not, in and of itself, disqualify you 
from participating in decisions before the Commission. Rather, you would be disqualified only from 
decisions that have a foreseeable and material financial effect on your personal financial interests as 
discussed below. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Under Regulation 18700(d)( 1 ), we must first consider if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
government decision will have a financial effect on an official's financial interests. Generally, a 
financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is "explicitly involved" in a 
decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) An interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party 
in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official's agency. (Ibid.) A financial 
interest is the subject of a governmental decision if it involves the issuance, renewal, approval, 
denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial 
interest. (Regulation 18701 (a).) 
Regulation 18701 (b) provides that if the interest is not explicitly involved in the government 
decision, the financial effect is reasonably foreseeable "if the financial effect can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility" and is '"more than hypothetical or theoretical." If the financial result cannot be 
expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not 
reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) Factors to consider in determining foreseeability are 
provided in Regulation 1870l(b) (1) through (6). 
Under Regulation 18700(d)(2), if there is a reasonably foreseeable financial effect, we then 
consider if the financial effect will be material. For a personal financial interest, a financial effect is 
material if the official will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision. 
(Regulation 18702.5.) Examples of a material financial effect for the personal finances of an 
owner/occupier of a mobilehome with a monthly rental space include: the increase or decrease in 
the value of the mobilehome; resale value of the mobilehome; increase or decrease in the cost/value 
of the space-rental; or costs associated with a move from a particular mobilehome park.3 
2 Note that an interest in a month-to-month lease is not a potentially disqualifying interest. (Regulation 18233.) 
J Note that an analysis of the effect of a government decision on your financial interest in your personal 
finances is fact•specific. Accordingly. we cannot analyze the potential effect any particular decision may have on your 
personal finances unless the nature of the decision has been identified. Should you need additional assistance 
detennining whether any particular decision related to mobilehomes may have a reasonably foreseeable material effect 
on your personal finances, you should seek further advice describing the nature of the decision. 


Mobilehome Park Protection Policies/Conversion Ordinance  
Given the current facts, the applicable foreseeability standard is whether a financial effect 
"can be recognized as a realistic possibility," and is "more than hypothetical or theoretical." 
(Regulation 1870l(b).) 
Here, where the City is seeking to strengthen its mobilehome park preservation and 
conversion policies, it is foreseeable that the City's action could result in changes to its current 
policies which could impact your mobilehome and your current mobilehome park. As noted above 
by City staff, the conversion of a mobilehome park most likely results in a mobilehome owner's 
complete loss of their home, and greater expenses in obtaining new housing. Thus, the policies 
could have a reasonably foreseeable, measurable financial benefit or loss to you. 
Mobilehome Rent Ordinance: Mobilehome "Opt-In/Stay in Business" matter 
Policy decisions relating to allowing mobilehome park owners who agree to remain in 
business for a minimum of 20 years to impose a monthly capital improvement pass-through fee, 
and/or change the base rents for a purchaser of a mobilehome could foreseeably impact your 
mobilehome and your current mobilehome park. Thus, the policies could have a reasonably 
foreseeable, measurable financial benefit or loss to you. 
Budget/fee approval; Mobilehome Rent Ordinance fees 
We do not have any information regarding a particular "budget/fee" decision such that we 
can analyze the effect on your personal finances. You have indicated that "fees are generally raised 
by a few dollars each year. No proposal yet." Your City Attorney has inquired "whether with 
respect to the budget/fee approval, the impact of the change in Mobilehome Rent Ordinance fees on 
your personal finances may be considered inconsequential or insignificant such that it does not lead 
to a conflict." 
You would be disqualified from such a fee decision if it has a foreseeable and material 
financial effect on your personal financial interests. However, Regulation l 8702(b) does provide 
that notwithstanding the materiality standards in Regulations 18702. l through 18702.5, the 
financial effect of a governmental decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or 
insignificant. Depending on what the impact of the change in fees on your personal finances is, it is 
possible that it could be considered nominal or inconsequential.
